# grokking

Grokking is the phenomenon where a neural network first fits its training set through [[memorization-vs-generalization|memorization]] — achieving near-perfect training accuracy — then, after a long plateau during which test performance remains low, abruptly transitions to genuine generalisation and performs well on unseen examples too. [[220102177b.pdf]] [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

## Original Experimental Evidence

[[220102177b.pdf]] demonstrates, on a 2-layer width-128 4-attention-head transformer (~400K non-embedding parameters) trained on [[algorithmic-datasets|binary operation tables]] over abstract discrete symbols, that validation accuracy can remain at chance level until roughly 1000× the number of optimisation steps at which training accuracy first reaches near-perfect. The canonical example is modular division mod 97 with 50% of the data as training: training accuracy converges before 10³ steps; validation accuracy does not reach that level until approximately 10⁶ steps, with little evidence of any generalisation until 10⁵ steps.

The surprising element is the delay. If a clean rule underlies the data, why doesn't gradient descent find it immediately? [[compuflair2026theph.md]] proposes, on modular arithmetic tasks (e.g. addition modulo 17 with ~40% of pairs shown during training), that the model first lands in a large memorisation basin and only later drifts into the narrower rule basin under the sustained pressure of [[weight-decay]] and optimisation noise.

## Data Efficiency Curves

[[220102177b.pdf]] demonstrates, on the abstract group S5 composition objective, that the number of optimisation steps required to reach 99% validation accuracy grows rapidly as the fraction of training data decreases. Near the critical data fraction (approximately 25–30% for S5), a 1% decrease in training data produces a 40–50% increase in median time to generalisation. The number of steps to reach 99% training accuracy, by contrast, generally decreases as dataset size shrinks and stays in the range of 10³–10⁴ steps across all conditions. [[220102177b.pdf]] observes this exponential scaling of optimisation time on all algorithmic tasks for which the networks could be made to generalise.

This creates a distinctive compute-data tradeoff: for small [[algorithmic-datasets|algorithmic datasets]], one can substitute additional optimisation for additional data, at the cost of an exponentially increasing compute budget.

## Learned Representations After Grokking

[[220102177b.pdf]] demonstrates, via t-SNE visualisation of output-layer weight vectors in networks that have grokked, that learned embeddings reflect the algebraic structure of the task's underlying mathematical objects. For [[modular-arithmetic|modular addition]] mod 97, the output weights form a circular arrangement, with a "number line" recoverable by repeatedly adding a fixed residue. For the symmetric group S5, the embeddings cluster into cosets of the subgroup generated by specific permutations. This structure is more apparent in networks optimised with [[weight-decay]], consistent with weight decay selecting for more structured, lower-norm solutions. [[220102177b.pdf]] suggests that such visualisations could offer a window into novel mathematical objects whose properties are not already known.

## Training Dynamics

Early in training, training loss drops quickly while test loss stays high. The model has memorised the training examples but not learned the underlying rule. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

During the plateau, training accuracy is already near-perfect, so the strong downhill gradient force is gone. What remains are weaker forces: the steady pull of regularisation and the gentle jitter of mini-batch noise. The model is still drifting internally, even though the training curve looks flat. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

The eventual transition can appear more abrupt than it is. Test *accuracy* (right/wrong) changes in steps, producing a cliff-like plot. Test *loss* — a smoother signal — typically shows a ramp rather than a jump. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

## Why It Matters

Grokking is simultaneously a warning and a promise. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

**Warning:** a model can look stable on training data for a long time while still relying on a shallow memorisation strategy that will not transfer. Stopping early — or evaluating only on non-adversarial examples — can give a false impression of understanding.

**Promise:** training longer under the right simplicity pressure can transform brittle memorisation into robust generalisation *without changing the architecture or adding new data*. The rule was latent in the network's capacity all along.

## Hyperparameter Sensitivity

Grokking is not a single-knob phenomenon. [[compuflair2026theph.md]] proposes it depends on the interaction among capacity, data, regularisation, and training dynamics:

**Batch size (temperature):** Small batches introduce more stochastic noise into the optimisation trajectory, analogous to higher temperature in a physical system. This can help the model explore and escape narrow grooves, but too much noise prevents settling into any basin. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

**Learning rate schedule (cooling):** Lowering the learning rate reduces exploration and freezes the model into whatever basin it currently occupies. Cooling too early can lock in memorisation. Cooling at the right time — after the rule basin has become favorable — can help the model settle cleanly into it. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

**Model size:** Larger models can memorise more easily, potentially extending the plateau. But they also have more capacity to represent the underlying rule. Under the right regularisation pressure they may ultimately grok more cleanly. [[compuflair2026theph.md]]

**[[weight-decay]] strength:** The most direct control knob. Too weak leaves memorisation stable indefinitely; too strong prevents training data from being fit at all. See [[weight-decay]] for the Goldilocks zone argument.
